JavaScript Core (64) - ES6: Arrow Functions - A Quick Intro
Time for a brand new chapter — arrow functions.
A Quick Intro to Arrow Functions
Arrow functions, also known as Arrow Functions (the English name), are technically expressions, so the proper name is actually arrow function expression. It's a piece of syntax that arrived with ES6.

Honestly, a lot of ES6 syntax is what we call syntactic sugar. What's syntactic sugar? Basically it's like candy — sweet and convenient. It makes writing and reading code easier, and the common traits are that it's shorter, handier, and nicer to read. For example, back in the day if you wanted to set up a prototype you had to write a whole pile of
syntax to make that easier. There's also genuinely new syntax in ES6, but that's not what this post is about, so let's get back to arrow functions.

With ES6 arrow functions, we can rewrite that as:
Arrow functions also have a shorthand ability. When the body is just an expression, you can drop the

const myName = (name) => 'myName is :' + name;

console.log(myName('Ray')); // myName is Ray
On top of that, when the arrow function has exactly one parameter, you can even drop the parentheses around it:

const myName = name => 'myName is :' + name;

console.log(myName('Ray')); // myName is Ray
Just watch out though — if there are no parameters, or two parameters, you can't drop the parentheses.

Time for a brand new chapter — arrow functions. This is a quick intro to the ES6 arrow function syntax: how it compares to a regular function expression, the shorthand tricks you can use to drop the return and the braces, and the one rule about when you can (and can't) skip the parameter parentheses.
